I think it's totally fine to do `sources=['sample-debian-package-debian-sources/**/*']` if the assumption is that all those sources need to be present as-is when you run the debian packaging command. That way Pants doesn't need to know anything about the structure, such as the existence of `DEBIAN/control` . So for example if in the future debian added some new way of providing the metadata, say `NEW_DEBIAN/new_control` , then things would still work. Not that they are likely to do that, but it illustrates that the less special-case knowledge Pants knows the better...

this was initially implemented consuming a `ProcessResult`, which used to output the fact that the process had failed including the contents of `stdout` and `stderr`. The reason this was changed is because this `render` operation was implemented as a helper rule to be able to evaluate the dependencies of a `helm_deployment`.

The idea is that, since Helm charts deploy containers, we could infer dependency relationships between a chart and other `docker_images` in the same repo. However charts are also templates in which (in some cases) even the actual containers that are going to be deployed can be overridden at deployment-time.

So dependency relationships between `helm_chart` and `docker_image` can not be safely determined. However we can do so between `helm_deployment` and `docker_image` since this one includes the information that is going to be used to render the templates.

All of this to say that to infer the dependencies we render the `helm_deployment` and then parse all the files in search for references to container images. Using `ProcessResult` with is _verbosy_ output during failures, gave the feeling of _Pants just exploded_ in this context since some users didn't expect a Helm command being run under the hood when doing (i.e.) `./pants dependencies path/to/helm:deployment`.

So the usage of `FallibleProcessResult` is an attempt at hiding the fact that a process has run under the hood and give a bit more of a user-friendly error message. In most of the other instances in which we run a command (i.e. inside a goal) we do use `ProcessResult`.

I modified this because `X.X.X` implies that the the version components are the same variable repeated (e.g. 1.1.1), whereas `X.Y.Z` makes it clear that they are three different variables, and that the two different uses of the X.Y.Z string _are_ equivalent. Furthermore, this is the most widely used standard convention (at least in English) for describing arbitrary version and tag names, e.g. in [SemVer](https://semver.org/) itself, and a search for `version "X.Y.Z"` gives 400 million hits for mostly version-related things, while `version "X.X.X"` gives you 600 million hits that are overwhelmingly dominated by...well, something that isn't very family-friendly (one more reason to avoid it, heh).

import operator

> (why) will this step be repeated if it's successful? is there a way to see if the step succeeded and not iterate?

There is no known to me way to determine if step was a success. In case of a real failure the step just does nothing - the list of repositories it needs to update is empty.

> is the operation idempotent (in the event you cannot determine if the step succeeded and are forced to do the step several times)?

I needed to look in Wikipedia what [Idempotence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idempotence) actually is and found out that yes, the operation is idempotent.

> What kind of outages is the backoff strategy aiming to mitigate and what is the duration and cause of those outages? 

The repository object can not be fetched from a [cache](https://github.com/jenkinsci/github-plugin/blob/26ac9b0d1c4702f5d0940201a7e8deb19d5a42c6/src/main/java/com/cloudbees/jenkins/GitHubRepositoryName.java#L163-L167) for some reason by [GitHubCommitStatusSetter](https://github.com/jenkinsci/github-plugin/blob/497f43251e3bfc130f125f75e45f73e06894361d/src/main/java/org/jenkinsci/plugins/github/status/GitHubCommitStatusSetter.java). This happens once in a while and became a significant problem because we have split the pipelines. 

> Is the backoff of 1 second x 3 empirically effective? Have you considered different strategies? 

We are aiming to test that on all the PR's. For the test job it didn't fail at all. If this simple strategy will not be enough we might look into other.

> is there a more robust way to handle this planned?

Potentially [another](https://wiki.jenkins.io/display/JENKINS/GitHub+Integration+Plugin) plugin might be used when it will be mature enough. Currently it doesn't work since it can not be configured properly.

Short answer: no. Part of the problem is that `has_role` is a `PersonEntity` method, while `nb_persons` is a `GroupEntity` method. The data that is being reduced belongs to `GroupEntity`.

I can't see a way to solve this without first addressing the problems in `has_role` - specifically the fact that it requires access to all group entities via the Simulation instance.

This is partially a consequence of the signature for formulas - you get a `person` but not the other entities so that `person` is the only thing you can call `has_role` on. And implicitly, `has_role` takes **two** arguments, not one; you don't ask "what role a person has" generically, you're always asking "what role does a person fill in a particular group". The problem is that the Role values are too lightweight (they're [flyweights in the GoF patterns sense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flyweight_pattern)) to carry this information.

So the underlying question is, what better approach can we find for filtering by role, and how does this affect the signature of formulas… which is way out of scope for this PR.

How about `KeyError` because of `_SCALAR_DESERIALIZATION_FUNCTIONS[expected_type.name]`? Is that a bug in the code, or a user input error?

You could avoid this by applying a variant of the "parse, don't validate" principle: https://lexi-lambda.github.io/blog/2019/11/05/parse-don-t-validate/

You have chosen to call `_validate_json_scalar_argument()` which returns no arguments, and then use that as an implicit "proof" that `_SCALAR_DESERIALIZATION_FUNCTIONS[expected_type.name]` won't raise `KeyError`. In addition to being implicit and therefore worse than explicit, this code is also not particularly stable in the face of refactoring and code evolution.

Instead, make a new function instead of `_validate_json_scalar_argument()` that has to either raise an error or return a deserializer function, which you can then call inside the `try-except`. That way, this new function has to do the potentially-KeyError-producing step, but in that case KeyError is an obvious bug due to a failure to appropriately check the user's input.

Maybe we should draw Venn diagrams: Awkward has high-level ListType (ListArray and ListOffsetArray) and RegularType, which can contain anything; Arrow has one variable-length list type and Tensor, for the special case of regular arrays of numbers. If the Awkward structure in question is a NumpyArray with len(shape) > 1 or a NumpyArray nested within RegularArrays, then it can go to Tensor. Anything else can't and has to defer to Arrow's list type.

For Awkward RegularArrays (containing anything other than RegularArrays and NumpyArrays), that means generating regular offsets, as you say. For Awkward ListArrays, that means "compacting" the list because Arrow uses offsets, not starts and stops. Fortunately, all three of these classes have a `toListOffsetArray64` method that normalizes the data (to an Arrow LargeListArray because it's 64-bit, and we could add a `toListOffsetArray32` in the future if it's useful to make smaller Arrow buffers).

About masking, it's true that something like `RegularArray(BitMaskedArray(RegularArray(NumpyArray(...))))` can't be expressed as an Arrow Tensor because the option-type is in the middle. It represents data like `[None, [2, 3]]`, which is not something an Arrow Tensor can represent. One option is to push all the Nones to the leaf level, like `[[None, None], [2, 3]]`, which is what `ak.to_numpy` does (producing a NumPy MaskedArray). But it's also okay to punt and just say that only structures like `RegularArray(RegularArray(BitMaskedArray(NumpyArray(...))))` are allowed.

(Or am I wrong and Arrow's Tensor only takes masks at the outside, like `BitMaskedArray(RegularArray(RegularArray(NumpyArray(...))))`? If so, that would be strange because it's the opposite of NumPy.)

1) Move the validation into `compute_value`, which will make this validation eager when the value is first parsed (eager validation is good, I argue):

```python
PYTHON_INTERPRETER_REGEX = r"python(?P<major>\d)\.(?P<minor>\d+)"

@classmethod
def compute_value(cls, raw_value: Optional[str], *, address: Address) -> str:
   value = cast(str, super().compute_value(raw_value, address=address)
   if not re.match(cls.PYTHON_INTERPRETER_REGEX, value):
      raise InvalidFieldException("")
   return value
```

def convert_to_interpreter(self) -> Tuple[int, int]:
   mo = re.match(cls.PYTHON_INTERPRETER_REGEX, self.value)

@brandon-rhodes I've found an acceptable implementation of `sgp4init()` which leaves the variables as read-only, exposes additional (read-only) variables for convenience with **one key exception** - the epoch-related variables:
1. satrec.jdsatepoch
1. satrec.jdsatepochF
1. satrec.epochyr
1. satrec.epochdays

When a `Satrec()` is initialized with twoline2rv, these variables are set/derived from the epoch data in the TLE - giving a convenient internal reference for what epoch the `Satrec` is associated with. 

Vallado's `sgp4init()` offers no equivalent, and no internal functions with which to call in order to derive the variables - the SGP4epoch -> MJD epoch is easy enough, but converting either of those to a year and DOY are not possible, as there is not an inverse of `days2mdhms()`

Since we're treating the Vallado C-code as "read-only", I would propose allowing the above 4 variables to be writable such that `sgp4init()` users can create a workflow and structure equivalent to `twoline2rv()` calls (if they care to).  This would appear to be safe, as these variables are only set and referenced by `twoline2rv()`, but not used by any other SGP4 function.

`twoline2rv()` would automatically overwrite anything with variables consistent with the provided TLE, and anyone who is using them via `sgp4init()` would have had to take the explicit action to set them as well.

Before pylint supported named warnings, my template for test files started with a block that turned off the most common false-positive warnings for tests and also described what was being turned off:

```python
# disable: accessing protected members, too many methods
# pylint: disable=W0212,R0904
```

For test files, I personally still tend to do that if, for whatever reason, a particular test file is generating a lot of the same spurious warning. There's no hard and fast rule, but usually if the same warning occurs more than maybe 5 times I put it at the top, otherwise I prefer to comment each problem point. Or, if it's a warning that I feel is actually valid and should be fixed, I'll still comment at each point. Only innocuous warnings go in the block at the top.

```python
# pylint:disable=too-many-lines,no-member,protected-access
```

Fair call. However, the problem, I think, is architectural. 
The seed_aug parameter is passed in to the default image augmenter (https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/io/image_aug_default.cc#L101). But, the actual random number generator is external, but seeded internally (https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/io/image_aug_default.cc#L254).

As I've explained above, I can't see how to meet the requirements of processing the images in parallel and ensuring that the output is reproducible (by setting the augmentation seed). 

My fix for the flakiness (without violating the aforementioned requirements), is to ensure that the RNG used to process the image is seeded before processing the image within that thread it is processed in.

To be clear, none of the changes made were to facilitate running the tests, but to actually correct the underlying problem. > Speaking from anecdotal experience most of the people that I know who use datasets from torchvision don't even look at the training reference scripts / know that they exist.

Ah, that's a shame. Perhaps this is because we didn't do a great job at documenting these in the past. Hopefully the new doc revamp will help raising awareness about them.

> Dataset centric way or Task centric way

I agree with your analysis. For OF datasets we opted for the dataset-centric way. I don't think that there is a clear winner **a-priori**, but now that we already went this way for OF datasets, honouring consistency is critical.

> To me personally, transforms imply modifying / altering data, not necessarily creating or extracting information and as a personal preference I'd avoid using transforms that transform data types / generate information.

I tend to agree as well. We still decided to generate the fake data in the transforms rather in the datasets, because generating it in the datasets has various drawbacks:

- it bounds to user to *one* specific generation, which may not be the one they want. For example in optical flow we generate a fake `valid_mask` depending on a given threshold, but the default threshold may not suit all users:

  https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/1dd17538d98f960bcdfa35cab7fd51efbc73cbbf/references/optical_flow/transforms.py#L33:L33

   (we could make this a parameter of the dataset class, but it's not ideal either)

- it  generates a lot of useless data and thus uses memory, while some users may not actually need the `valid_mask` at all. So it's best to avoid generating it on their behalf.

> Another argument that I have for consistent shape outputs from getitem is such that you can guarantee transform associativity as often as possible.

I agree this is important, and we can have that regardless of the design :) In the OF datasets, we don't return (or generate fake) values that don't exist, but we still require all the transforms to have the same signature: `img1, img2, flow, valid_mask`:
-  https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/add-crestereo-dataset/torchvision/datasets/_optical_flow.py#L65:L65
- https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/1dd17538d98f960bcdfa35cab7fd51efbc73cbbf/torchvision/datasets/_optical_flow.py#L242-L245

the thing is that DeclarativeMeta exists also in sqlalchemy2 and it possible to declare the model the old way as well (using declarative_base()). 

So the old way works in sqlalchemy2 and sqlalchemy1
```
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
Base = declarative_base()
class User(Base):
    ...
```

The new way is only in sqlalchemy2:
```
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase
class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    ...

class User(Base):
    ...
```

Your proposed change will successfully import DeclarativeBase from sqlalchemy2 and fail checking instance against DeclarativeBase if it was declared the old way (using declarative_base()).

I'm thinking we may use the results of the write tasks to indicate if the entire write operation is complete or failed this way:
- The write tasks can have an output anyway: i.e. the `transform_fn` will be mapping `Iterator[Blocks]` to `WriteResult`. If the task succeeds, `WriteResult` is "ok", otherwise it's the error object from the task.
- The `write_xx()` still returns None, but internally it will run the plan with the write as the last operator; the results of executing that plan is a Dataset comprised of `List[WriteResult]`.
- We don't expose this dataset `List[WriteResult]` from `write_xx()`, but internally use it as an indicator to run these two callbacks: if all elements are "ok", `on_write_complete`, otherwise `on_write_failed`.

This is similar to the argument which has underpinned the decision to ignore `DeprecationWarning` by default in the interpreter.

While I respect this position, I have a significant concern about this is how it impacts users who are writing relatively small scripts and have no testing mechanisms. Such a user will typically _never_ run the interpreter with `-W` filters specified.

`Warning` as the base + the toggle env var ensures that these users will see the warnings when we flip the default for the env var flag.
The big alternative I see is to use `DeprecationWarning` as the base and remove the env var (why bother suppressing the warning explicitly if it's a `DeprecationWarning`?).

i.e. I think that for our user-base, the developer/user distinction is less clear than it is with some other libraries. We can and should discuss this further. I could be convinced, in part because there's a question of how often and why/when scripting users would be updating to the newest library version. And the end-goal is to have the smoothest upgrade experience possible.

Unfortunately this doesn't really work because `Iterable[T]` and `Mapping[t.Any, T]` overlap.
`pyright` seems to pick the less specific, so in this case if we pass a dict, the `Iterable[T]` will be picked and `T` will be the keys' type instead of the values' type.
`mypy` rejects this type altogether, passing a dict, we get an incompatible type error, not exactly sure why.

When I first started typing this library I used a generic type alias for the iteratee type which reduced overload amount a lot. If we pick `drop_while` as a simple example. Current type is:
```python
@t.overload
def drop_while(array: t.List[T], predicate: t.Callable[[T, int, t.List[T]], t.Any]) -> t.List[T]:
    ...


@t.overload
def drop_while(array: t.List[T], predicate: t.Callable[[T, int], t.Any]) -> t.List[T]:
    ...


@t.overload
def drop_while(array: t.List[T], predicate: t.Callable[[T], t.Any]) -> t.List[T]:
    ...


@t.overload
def drop_while(array: t.List[T], predicate: None = None) -> t.List[T]:
    ...
```

And with my generic type alias idea I had:
```python
F = t.Union[
    t.Callable[[T, T2, t.List[T]], T3],
    t.Callable[[T, T2], T3],
    t.Callable[[T], T3],
    None,
]

def drop_while(array: t.List[T], predicate: F[T, int, t.Any] = None):
    ...
```

Which is really nice, you can reduce a lot of overload, and it works for list and dict, depending on the generic values you pass. Here `int` as second because it is a list, for mapping it would be:

```python
F = t.Union[
    t.Callable[[T, T2, t.Dict[T2, T]], T3],
    t.Callable[[T, T2], T3],
    t.Callable[[T], T3],
    None,
]

def some_func_accepting_dict(d: t.Mapping[T2, T], predicate: F[T, T2, t.Any] = None):
    ...
```

This is a bit different than the `sort_by` example above as you still need to have overloads for `Iterable` and `Mapping` but it combines all the different predicate/iteratee overloads in one single overload.

While this works perfectly fine with `pyright`, it doesn't work at all with `mypy`, it just doesn't get it somehow and everything is typed as `Any`. I opened an issue in `mypy` about this when I realized: https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/14498

it's better to pass generator object to `any` instead of a list:
```python
any(condition(x) for x in some_list) # generator
any([condition(x) for x in some_list]) # list
```
when we're working with a list we should instantiate the whole of it, so calculate the condition for all elements in `some_list` and after that call `any` on the resulted list. When we're working with a generator we don't need to instantiate the whole sequence, after the first `True` condition `any` can consider that it should return `True`.

Check this example:
```python
>>> def condition(x):
...     print(f"called on {x}")
...     return True if x >= 1 else False
...
>>> any(condition(x) for x in range(5))
called on 0
called on 1
True
>>> any([condition(x) for x in range(5)])
called on 0
called on 1
called on 2
called on 3
called on 4
True
```

The assert holds because?
Generally tricky with these deep assumptions

> In the case of a no-op preview step, the old input state is copied to the new input state. As a result,
any resource refs are marshaled/unmarshaled in the SDK code. ComponentResources are not guaranteed
to register resources until the RegisterOutputResources method has been called, which leads to a race
condition where the SDK invokes the getResource method to resolve a resource reference prior to
the resource being ready/registered.

I'd rephrase as follows:

"In the case of a `SameStep`, the old resource outputs are returned to the language host after the step is executed. The outputs of a `ComponentResource` may depend on resources that have not been registered at the time the `ComponentResource` is itself registered, as the outputs are set by a later call to `RegisterResourceOutputs`. Therefore, when the `SameStep` returns the old resource outputs for a `ComponentResource`, it may return references to resources that have not yet been registered, which will cause the SDK's calls to `getResource` to fail when it attempts to resolve those references.

> One potential fix would be to change the behavior of pulumi:pulumi:getResource to block until a register resource for the URN comes in (failing if the deployment completes without the URN being seen).

This seems dangerous, as it could trivially lead to deadlock (i.e. the program is waiting for a resource that is never registered, thus keeping the deployment from ever completing). I think that the right long-term fix is to change the behavior of `SameStep` to only return old outputs for custom resources. In theory, the language SDK shouldn't even need the old outputs for a component resource--it simply deserializes them and discards them.

the approach of passing the schema and converter to the constructor seems defensible, but...

My feeling is still to err on the side of simplicity. Primarily, I'm struggling to see the value of the Converter idea vs just requiring you need to pass in a "dict convertible like object" (i.e. one with a `to_dict()` method) into the produce method otherwise it blows up. The latter seems easier to grok (one less abstraction), and pythonic. I'd would be convinced to change my opinion on this if there's precedent from other well used python libraries (i don't know, don't use them).

An argument against this is if people typically don't want to/can't have a `to_dict` method on their types they want to produce. I feel most of the time this won't be a problem and if it is, it's a simple matter to provide the behavior in a sub class. Also, if it turns out people do do the subclass thing all the time, the `Converter` concept can be added later. On the flip side of this, if we add it now, we can't take it away. Err on the side of smallest API is a good strategy. 

Second, I was wondering if even the record_schema could be specified via a `.schema()` method on the object passed into the produce method. That I think is not a good idea though for reasons of performance, and also what most closely matches the behavior in other languages is to specify it in the constructor (in other languages it's a static property of the type... but close).

While I think such a cmake option works and would exlcude the `tcplp` library from being included, it indirectly adds a restriction in that `TCP` under cmake can now only be enabled using this option.

In OT we have different ways of enabling features and providing the `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_{}` definitions.
- We can use the build specific option, like cmake options above.
- Or a project can provide a project-specific config header, which includes all `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_{}` definitions it wants and pass it as config header (e.g. in cmake using `OT_CONFIG`).

The model typically used for other cmake options is that they can enable a feature (set the related `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_{}` to `1`), but won't explicitly disable it (wont explicitly set it to `0`). 

This way if the cmake option the is not specified/used, it allows the project specific config header to be used and specify the features to enable. 

----

I understand the main goal is to restrict inclusion of `tcplp` in `target_link_libraries`. Here is my suggestion for one possible way to realize this:
- We can add a new cmake option, `OT_EXCLUDE_TCPLP_LIB`
  - If it is used, we then exlude the `tcplp` including in link libs.
  - By default it is not set and therefore we include `tcplp`

By adding a different option for this (and not use `OT_TCP`) we keep these two independent of each other which allow a project-specific config header to be used under `cmake`. 

Thoughts?

This kind of thing is numerically unstable, in the same way that `log(1+exp(x))` is unstable. If the inputs are not small, exp() will produce inf which will then convert to NaN in the next lines, causing training to fail.  This kind of thing needs to be written in a way that doesn't cause values of `d` with modest values (e.g. a `d` of 5000) to yield NaN.

Take a look at the graph of this function: https://www.wolframalpha.com/input/?i=derivative+of+x*tanh%28log%281%2Bexp%28x%29%29%29. Note how the gradient saturates at 1 and 0.  Again, you can use if statements similar to how `dlib::log1pexp` handles this kind of thing to make the gradient computation numerically stable.

> * Can `infinite_sheds.csv` be removed?  As far as I can tell it's not used for anything.

Yes. This is output of an Excel version that @mikofski has, but the algorithm here has deviated from what was done in the spreadsheet and so `infinite_sheds.csv` doesn't provide known output.

> * Doesn't the term "infinite sheds" apply equally well to the other popular 2D VF models?  I wonder if we should call this model something more specific.

Open for suggestions. This code isn't exactly what is done in the PVSC paper, because the published technique doesn't handle horizontal arrays, and this code doesn't use averaged view factors at one step (I don't recall which).

> A comment about the model itself: the assumption of isotropic sky diffuse irradiance seems a little regrettable for this level of model detail. Especially for tracking systems, the difference between isotropic and anisotropic models for front-side sky diffuse can be quite large, even roughly the same magnitude as the entire rear-side irradiance:

Fair point. And an excellent illustration of the value of hosting multiple bifacial irradiance models in pvlib.

Any model that is computing incoming sky diffuse using the difference of cosine view factor is implicitly assuming that the incoming irradiance is isotropic. The alternative (separating sky diffuse into circumsolar, horizon and rest-of-sky) requires view factors that are aware of elevation angle, and a different interface (e.g. `sky_diffuse_horizon` would be an input, or the code would have to provide a selection of diffuse POA models). Neither is appealing here, either for scope, or to not depart farther from the reference than we already have.

The differences you plot between `infinite_sheds` and `pvfactors` could also be affected algorithmic differences: `pvfactors` is accounting for reflections from all nearby surfaces, where `infinite_sheds` only accounts for ground reflections.

I use a concept of using <> for external headers (STL, 3-RD party) and "" for internal headers

For #include <filename> the preprocessor searches in an implementation dependent manner, normally in search directories pre-designated by the compiler/IDE. This method is normally used to include standard library header files.

For #include "filename" the preprocessor searches first in the same directory as the file containing the directive, and then follows the search path used for the #include <filename> form. This method is normally used to include programmer-defined header files.

We try to avoid using the word "scan" to mean "run" because:

* Some experiments aren't really scanning anything, and people in the past have been confused on that point.
* Sometimes a run is scanning multiple axis, such as `grid_scan`, so wording like this can be confusing.

"Run" is a pretty vague term, but we have never come up with a better one that sufficiently conveys how general it is. This language the [the event-model documentation on our Data Model](https://blueskyproject.io/event-model/data-model.html) may be useful:

> The scope of a “run” is left to the instrument team or individual scientist. It is quite generic: a set of Documents generated by following a given sequence of instructions. This might encompass a single short measurement (say, “a count”) or a multi-step procedure such as a raster scan. A run should represent a set of individual measurements that will be collected or processed together.

I think every instance of the word "scan" here and below should be changed to "run" or "plan", depending on the situation.

Yes, the problem is that we either have to have a rule that the command arguments override the environment variables or vice versa. The rule was (and I guess I could change this because it isn't clear it's right) that the arguments override the env variables, and so putting False here made the env variables not work. But anyway there is logic that picks up the env variables and defaults it. (This is all heavily unit tested but could still have gone by you as even then it's subtle.)

Another complexity in the design space is that env variables are strings and command arguments can be real datatypes. That's weird because when the env variables are merged, you also have to do various kinds of coercions to even get them on a level playing field, and THEN you have to flatten them again to strings to put them in an ini file and AGAIN recover them as strings later. I REALLY wish we were using a json structure to hold this data so we didn't have to do that second coerce-to-string.

```
def deserialize_json_value(json_value: Union[str, int, ...], expected_type: GraphQLType):
      ....
```
Since you can't pass  a `Union[str, ...]` argument into a function with a `str` type annotation, we would probably have to have a lot of `coerce_type` internally within this function. 

I am thinking of a similar scenario for the `serialize` function. We use the `serialize` value for validation. In the future, we might update the `validate_argument_type` type annotation to:

```
ALLOWED_PYTHON_TYPES: Union[str, int, ...]

def validate_argument_type(
       name: str, 
       value: ALLOWED_PYTHON_TYPES, 
       graphql_type: GraphQLType
)  -> None:
```

For a similar reason, we would have to a lot of `coerce_type` calls internally to make this function pass mypy linting.

Let's have a try catch on `pyarrow.from_uri`, and print this message for users, but then immediately raise the original error as well. This will help with the fact that the pyarrow from uri error message is pretty vague.


Ex:

```python

try:
    from_uri(storage_path)
except Exception as e:
    raise ValueError(
        "Your storage path is most likely invalid for one of the following reasons: "
        "(1) custom fs + a storage path with a prefix or (2) path is not absolute. "
        "See the error above for exact details."
    ) from e
```

In case you're unfamiliar with `pytest.raises()`, here are the docs:  https://docs.pytest.org/en/7.1.x/reference/reference.html#pytest-raises

Taking the chance to elaborate on my comment here: https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/1373#discussion_r224106008

I think as a rule of thumb: APIs *taking parmeters* (e.g. `def do_something(things: Iterable[Foo])` should accept the most flexible interface that the API can work with. I can hardly think of a reason why an API should constrain a parameter to a concrete type (e.g. `Tuple[Foo]`) if there is nothing intrinsically preventing that API from working with an `Iterable[Foo]`.

However, **Return types** should *usually* be concrete. Because, most of the time an API returning *something*, does have intrinsic knowledge about the type anyway and there is nothing to gain from making the return type more abstract if that return type fulfills that abstract interface anyway.

E.g. There is nothing to gain from doing this:

```python
def get_foo() -> Iterable[Foo]
    return [foo, too]
```

Even though that works, we are not improving flexibility here. If we make the return type `List[Foo]`, that `List[Foo]` still fulfills being `Iterable[Foo]` as well as `Sequence[Foo]` implicitly, so by making the return type somthing like `Iterable[Foo]` when in reality we have a `List[Foo]` is in fact only limiting the flexibility here.

Coming back to the code in question `shards_and_committees` is, after all, just a property on `BlocksCommitteesInfo`, hence making it fall in the return type bucket.

Related: [Covariance and Contravariance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Covariance_and_contravariance_(computer_science))

After studying the link supplied by @larroy (thanks!), I need to retract what I said above.  Based on a new understanding, I have removed the runtime check of the cuda-runtime library version.  The check is unnecessary since (per the link) the major.minor of the cuda runtime must match for the libmxnet.so lib to load.  It was instructive for me to do a `ldd libmxnet.so`:
```
	libcudart.so.9.2 => /usr/local/cuda/lib64/libcudart.so.9.2 (0x00007f361cc64000)
	libcudnn.so.7 => /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn.so.7 (0x00007f35f9cdf000)
	libcuda.so.1 => /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcuda.so.1 (0x00007f35f3703000)
```
Note the extra '.minor' number on libcudart.so.  So while a compiled-against cudnn 7.2, might run against a cudnn 7.6, a compiled-against cuda 10.1 won't run against a cuda 10.2.  Now, keep in mind we're talking about the cuda `runtime` library, so libcudart.so as set up by the toolkit install.  Your experience on 10.1 vs. 10.2 @KellenSunderland was probably based on upgrading the driver to a higher version, while leaving the toolkit install the same.

Let me know if the PR is now to your liking.  I've left in the test of the cuda runtime version against the threshold MXNET_CI_OLDEST_CUDA_VERSION.  The idea is that once we no longer test against a particular cuda version, then bugs will creep in with new PRs.  We'd prefer users to not be on the front line of bug finding, so we should encourage them to upgrade.

Back to your original question- there will be no warning for upgrading the driver to a newer version (e.g. 10.2) while leaving the toolkit at 10.1

Note that `NoReturn` is the "uninhabited type" (the type with no values) which is a subtype of everything. You don't have to use it in return position. And since it's a subtype of everything and Outcome is covariant in its value type, `Error` is a subtype of `Outcome[NoReturn]` which is a subtype of `Outcome[T]` for any T. So an Error can be provided where any type of Outcome is expected -- which is exactly what you want.

The semantics are currently very conservative, where we tell the `Batcher` to ensure that batches are always fresh copies (if we're creating batches, i.e. `batch_size is not None`):
* https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/8f823cb51b8aee1e1fa8e351c70448d14bf771f5/python/ray/data/dataset.py#L570
* https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/8f823cb51b8aee1e1fa8e351c70448d14bf771f5/python/ray/data/_internal/batcher.py#L109-L112

This may create redundant copies if either

1. the base block is already in the worker heap (e.g. due to read and map_batches fusion, or due to the block not supporting zero-copy deserialization), or,
2. the batch conversion will already create a data copy.

The all-encompassing solution would be explicitly checking if the batch is read-only and only then making a copy, but for batch formats like Pandas DataFrames, this is a bit difficult/expensive: we'd need to do an `O(blocks) = O(columns x chunks)` traversal of the DataFrame to get the underlying (ndarray) blocks, and check if each block is writable. This appears to only be possible by using a lot of internal Pandas APIs, which will be super fragile:
```python
if all(block.array_values.to_numpy().flags["WRITEABLE"] for block in batch._data.blocks):
    # No copy needed.
else:
    # Copy needed.
```
This is less fragile if we converted the top-level columns to ndarrays and checked the `"WRITEABLE"` flag for those ndarrays, but this would often trigger block consolidation to create contiguous column ndarrays, resulting in a copy of the DataFrame just to check if we can get away with not copying the DataFrame, which is obviously a non-starter.

 1. through `AddMethod()` with the code as string; this works for any programming language (even at the shell), and is meant for dynamically shaping mocks in a project's unit tests.
 2. declaring a proper D-Bus function in the template [like here](https://github.com/martinpitt/python-dbusmock/blob/master/dbusmock/templates/logind.py#L82); this is for writing non-trivial methods in templates.

The avro producer isn't actually allowing for serializer overrides so much as it's allowing you to optionally introduce a new datum writer by providing a new schema. Either way this is an example of the serializer's responsibility leaking into the producer itself which is not what we want. Instead the serializer should handle this. What makes this weird for Avro is that the python implementation does not actually have this notion of `Specific` or even `Generic` record types. If you want a record you pass a dict with no schema tied to it. That said this is a new class, and a new `GenericRecord` serializer(and supporting GenericRecord type (binding schema to dict)  can also be introduced 
 to handle this. In the end this will make for a much better Avro experience for everyone while also removing all these checks from the produce path.

alright, I tried a different approach in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4133/commits/05303c813131aeb614cd0b2c200946a829e76f4c. I think this will be more reliable. Instead of re-running `_find_random_port()` all at once for all workers again, the process will be like:

1. run `_find_random_port()` for every worker
2. if any duplicate IP-port pairs were found, run `_find_random_port()` again only for those workers that need to be changed to eliminate duplicates

I think that pattern (only re-running for the workers that need to be changed to resolve duplicates) should give us confidence that duplicates will be resolved, because the system time will change each time that function is run.

I think this approach, will still relies on `_find_random_port()`, is preferable to just incrementing and checking if the new port is open, because it should (on average) find a new open port more quickly than the incrementing approach. Consider the case where, for example, LightGBM tries to put multiple workers in a `LocalCluster` on port `8887` (1 less than the default port for Jupyter). Jupyter uses such an approach of "increment by one until you find an open port", so if someone has multiple Jupyter sessions running it's possible that they might have ports 8888, 8889, 8890, and 8891 all occupied (for example), which would mean LightGBM would need 5 attempts to find a new open port (if 8892 is open).

I think the existence of systems like this is why Dask also searches randomly if the default port it prefers for its scheduler is occupied when you run `dask-scheduler` (instead of incrementing). You can see that in the logs for LightGBM's Dask tests that use `dsitributed.LocalCluster`, for example from [this build](https://dev.azure.com/lightgbm-ci/lightgbm-ci/_build/results?buildId=9628&view=logs&j=c28dceab-947a-5848-c21f-eef3695e5f11&t=fa158246-17e2-53d4-5936-86070edbaacf):

This doesn't support polygon with inner rings. 

How about we use the following format with the key `coordinates`?

**Polygon with single ring** (The way you implemented.)
```
coordinates : [
     [ [ 2 , 2 ] , [ 3 , 3 ] , [ 4 , 2 ] , [ 2 , 2 ] ]
  ]
```

**Polygon with multiple rings** (The one that is not supported at the moment.)
```
coordinates : [
     [ [ 0 , 0 ] , [ 3 , 6 ] , [ 6 , 1 ] , [ 0 , 0 ] ],
     [ [ 2 , 2 ] , [ 3 , 3 ] , [ 4 , 2 ] , [ 2 , 2 ] ]
  ]
```

From geojson specification
> The "coordinates" member must be an array of LinearRing coordinate arrays. For Polygons with multiple rings, the first must be the exterior ring and any others must be interior rings or holes. 

Please see [django documentation](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.1/ref/contrib/gis/geos/#polygon) for usage.

Then there are functions that accept only pinList (e.g. niDigital_ConfigureTimeSetDriveFormat) param - These functions accepts only pin lists.

>with nifgen.Session('dev1') as session:

BTW, I ran into this recently on a machine that didn't have Istanbul. I was tempted to just install it globally like this, but ideally, we'd always use the version of it installed in the local `node_modules` as specified in the `package.json`.

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/48a1d3c3cb624955df5f757866b41c5b0d662310/sdk/nodejs/package.json#L35

We do already update `PATH` in the `Makefile` to be able to find such tools inside `node_modules`...

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/48a1d3c3cb624955df5f757866b41c5b0d662310/sdk/nodejs/Makefile#L13

So the calls to `istanbul` in the Makefile *should* use the one inside `./node_modules/.bin`...

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/48a1d3c3cb624955df5f757866b41c5b0d662310/sdk/nodejs/Makefile#L46-L49

However, I couldn't get this to work on macOS... Despite us [setting](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/48a1d3c3cb624955df5f757866b41c5b0d662310/build/common.mk#L93) `SHELL := /bin/bash` in `common.mk` that we include in the Makefile (which is apparently necessary on macOS), the calls to `istanbul` would not work because it could not be found. It turns out, it's due to a bug in the version of `make` on macOS. https://stackoverflow.com/a/21709821. I was able to get it working by installing a more recent version of `make` via `brew install make` and then setting `export PATH=/usr/local/opt/make/libexec/gnubin:$PATH` in my `~/.bash_profile` to use that newer version of `make`. Now when I run `make`, it'll use the version of istanbul that was installed in `./node_modules` after a `make ensure`.

It's not that anaconda's features are found last. To be exact, PATH lookup proceeds as follows:

When the user enters a non-relative and non-fullpath command (e.g. `conda` as opposed to `/opt/conda/bin/conda` and as opposed to `../conda/bin/conda`, then the operating system searches for `conda` on `PATH`. This is literally the dumbest lookup you can imaging. PATH is split into an ordered list at `:` characters on Unix and at `;` characters on Windows. Then the OS appends the program name (e.g. `conda`) to each entry on this list in turn. If a file is found to exist at that location then it is executed and the procedure finishes. If it isn't found, then we move onto the next entry doing the same append-then-check procedure. You are right about activate not happening though and that's more important IMHO. Either we respect activate and promote nothing but activate or we get rid of it entirely. In the middle ground lies nothing but frustration and bug reports.

    be called by the certbot.display.util functions and the mock returned by
    that call will be used as the IDisplay object. This was done to simplify

    functions directly in test code should be preferred over using this
    function in the future.

    :returns: patch on the function used internally by certbot.display.util to
        get an IDisplay object
    :rtype: unittest.mock._patch

    """

For example, prior to this change,`az network -h --verbose` yields:

```
(ext-venv) [12:39 PM] Work-Mac:azure-cli-wktree tosin$ az network -h --verbose
There is no applicable data for loader <class 'azure.cli.core._help_loaders.HelpLoaderV1'>.
There is no applicable data for loader <class 'azure.cli.core._help_loaders.HelpLoaderV1'>.
There is no applicable data for loader <class 'azure.cli.core._help_loaders.HelpLoaderV1'>.
     ...
     ...
There is no applicable data for loader <class 'azure.cli.core._help_loaders.HelpLoaderV1'>.
There is no applicable data for loader <class 'azure.cli.core._help_loaders.HelpLoaderV1'>.
There is no applicable data for loader <class 'azure.cli.core._help_loaders.HelpLoaderV1'>.

Group
    az network : Manage Azure Network resources.

Subgroups:
    application-gateway : Manage application-level routing and load balancing services.
    asg                 : Manage application security groups (ASGs).
    ddos-protection     : Manage DDoS Protection Plans.
    dns                 : Manage DNS domains in Azure.
    express-route       : Manage dedicated private network fiber connections to Azure.
    ...
    public-ip           : Manage public IP addresses.
    route-filter        : (PREVIEW) Manage route filters.
    route-table         : Manage route tables.
    ...
    ...
    vnet-gateway        : Use an Azure Virtual Network Gateway to establish secure, cross-premises

    vpn-connection      : Manage VPN connections.
    watcher             : Manage the Azure Network Watcher.

Commands:
    list-usages         : List the number of network resources in a region that are used against a
                          subscription quota.

```

**However**,  if we count the number of commands/groups under network, including  network itself (presence of ":" is an approximation)...
(ext-venv) [12:39 PM] Work-Mac:azure-cli-wktree tosin$ az network -h |grep ":"|  wc

and  count the number of logger info statements...
(ext-venv) [12:39 PM] Work-Mac:azure-cli-wktree tosin$ az network -h --verbose 2>&1 | grep "_help_loaders.HelpLoaderV1" | wc
      23     230    2254

We see they are the same (the error in the first wc is because there are two extra colons in the help text) So the count should be 22. There is an off by one error between what is loaded and counted because network has a deprecated command group that isn't displayed (`network interface-endpoint`).

I'm not sure about it. The point with the regular expression is that no replacement (removal) is done if the path is, for any reason, not a child of output_path. In order to do so with `os.path`, it seems that a helper function needs to be written. This is because, [relpath](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.realpath) will return a relative path, even if the test path is not a subset of output path. Therefore, some combination with [commonprefix](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.commonprefix) is required. However, `commonprefix` is buggy, and a better solution was introduced in Python 3.5: [commonpath](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.commonpath). See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7287996/python-get-relative-path-from-comparing-two-absolute-paths. Alternatively, in Python 3.4 pathlib was added https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html.

Overall, I think it is not worth implementing a solution based on `os.path` which is compatible with both Python 2.7 and Python >=3.5. We'd better keep the simple regex based approach and revisit this when Python 2 is deprecated. What do you think?

This method is used in refreshing a snapshot asynchronously. After we have processed this , the next web socket message could have a lastUpdateId <= this ID. This is handled in the `_check_update_id` method, which seemed to have changed in the last few weeks to remove a clause that allowed us to ignore such messages. Without the clause, this case would result in the order book being reset as the system would think it dropped a message. Setting the `delta` as None allows us to fall on the following if statement:
```
if self._l2_book[std_pair].delta is None and msg['u'] <= self.last_update_id[std_pair]:
    return True
```

Tbh it's a hack and probably a better idea would be to add a clause such as:
```
if msg['U'] <= self.last_update_id[std_pair] and msg['u'] <= self.last_update_id[std_pair]:
    return True
```

It feels like if both the start and end IDs are smaller than our last seen ID, we would be able to ignore that message safely. What do you think? I'd prefer removing this line and adding the above check instead.

`load_best` goes to some effort to match the parameter names in the current model, ignoring the new ones and leaving old ones unchanged. I believe this behaviour will be the same in this code.

During this process, `load_best` sets an "undefined" vector for the model so that it knows which parameters are not included in the loaded .par file. Within `init_pop` the undefined parameters will be initialized across their range using LHS irrespective of the population initializer set in the options.

The undefined parameters will stay randomized for all subsequent fits in the GUI until another parameter set is reloaded. This is probably not what the user wants. I think the simplest short-term fix is to remove `undefined` from the problem in `init_pop.generate`. At least then it will only affect the current fit, not all subsequent fits.

The idea is that you can randomize the parameter for the next round of fitting by commenting it out in the .par file. This was a horrible hack for a feature that is not advertised and therefore not used, except maybe by Joe if he remembers that it exists. Instead there should be explicit control of the randomized parameters on the command line and in the GUI. I added a ticket for this.

This whole block is unclear (note the excessive use of comments, that supports this finding), though it really does a very specific thing (I believe): Removing *behave-django* specific options.

There are two ways to solve this problem:

1. Use a list variable, e.g. `django_behave_specific_options`, and `if option in django_behave_specific_options:`, or
2. Rename the option to not use a `--behave-` prefix at all (e.g. `--bdd-test-runner` or just `--test-runner`).

Both solutions make the use of comments unnecessary.

Personally, I'd prefer the latter approach as this would reduce the amount of code needed and retain the clean mental model of "`--behave-` comes from *behave*".

1. We could add the variables and parameters to the pytest namespace, like:
```python
import pytest
import symfit as sf

def pytest_configure():
    pytest.x = sf.Variable('x')
    pytest.y = sf.Variable('y')
```
as `conftest.py` and the test example
```python
import pytest
import symfit as sf

def test_example():
   model = sf.Model({pytest.y: pytest.x})
   assert someting
```

2. We could use another file for the variables and parameters:
```python
import symfit as sf
x, y = sf.variables('x, y')
a, b, c = sf.parameters('a, b, c')
```
and import this file to use it
```python
from var import * # import the special file
import symfit as sf

def test_example():
   model = sf.Model({y: a*x**b+c})
   assert someting
```
Personally, I would prefer the second option, unless you know how to get `pytest.something` away :)

Maybe I did not make it clear in the previous comments, `Distribution` and `getF` both have nothing to do with `Block`, `HybridBlock` or `StochasticBlock`. A `Distribution` object can be used inside any types of blocks or even outside a block. The `getF` function is invoked in the initialization phase of a `Distribution` object to determine *whether to use `sym.np` or `nd.np` for the computation in the member function*, for example:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/18403/files#diff-e83cafb33610cdde004c72426d6d1492R82

In short, if the parameters are Symbols, then all the methods in the object will use function under the `sym.np` namespace. If the parameters are NDArray, then we'll choose to use functions under `nd.np`. 

I understand the possibility of `hybrid_forward` getting deleted in the future, but that deprecation hasn't reach community consensus, right? (https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/17676).

Furthermore, as I demonstrate in this test case: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/18403/files#diff-27fee32587466a570c77da139b7136f6R94-R96, current implementation works perfectly well with the new Gluon API (notice that `F` is not specified in the initialization function), that is to say, distribution object itself **does not rely on** the deprecated interface at all.

What I can do at this moment is:
- Create a new test file based on the new Gluon API. 
- Raise warning to discourage users from explicitly passing `F` when creating a distribution object.

Once `hybrid_forward` got officially deleted from the code base, I would then consider replacing all the `F` in this module with `mx.np` as well as removing `F` from the initialization function.

Per the conversation in #4192, https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/4425#issuecomment-872943848, and elsewhere, LightGBM tends to run fastest when `num_threads` is set to the number of *physical* (not *logical*) CPUs. For `lightgbm::lightgbm()`, I recommend changing this to `parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE)`.

That is kind of the point. The builder **silently** ignores wrong keyword arguments. Imagine you made a typo

```python
deeplabv3_resnet50(weight=DeepLabV3_ResNet50_Weights.CocoWithVocLabels_V1)
```

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/93b26da3623c9134d64f026a234d97caab0675f2/torchvision/prototype/models/vgg.py#L172-L179

will ultimately be resolved in 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/93b26da3623c9134d64f026a234d97caab0675f2/torchvision/models/vgg.py#L35-L38

Also, since this is a standard library feature test macro, since C++20, it has been moved to the `<version>` header. So we also need something like:

```
#if defined(__has_include)
#  if __has_include(<version>)
#    include <version>
# endif
#endif
```

Probably somewhere in `detail/common.h`.

And no, `defined(__has_include) && __has_include(<version>)` doesn't work, because old and crappy compilers get scared when they see that kind of thing.

Before anyone asks, yes, `<version>` is a must. I've had troubles with clang with `-std=c++20`.

Thank you for clarifying @DickJC123.

> I think most implementations of std::string will add 1 to the size you give at string construction time, because they want an efficient implementation of string::c_str(). So it would probably work, but why risk it?

As of C++11, the standard guarantees that "a pointer to s[0] can be passed to functions that expect a pointer to the first element of a null-terminated CharT[] array" according to https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/string/basic_string. Ie the pointer returned by `s.c_str()` must be the same as as the pointer to `s[0]`. So there would be no risk in allocating the string of true size and avoid the extra resize.

The reason I suggested `.isascii()` specifically is that thanks to a peculiarity of how strings are implemented, it can just look at an internal flag on the string object and so it takes O(1) time. Therefore, it's significantly faster than other similar-seeming methods like `isalpha()`, which in turn are quite a bit faster than running the regex engine. To demonstrate: ``` In [3]: COMMON_SINGLE_WIDTH_CHARS: Final = re.compile(r"^[\x20-\x7e\xa0-\u02ff]*$") In [4]: s = "a" * 40 In [5]: %timeit COMMON_SINGLE_WIDTH_CHARS.match(s) 229 ns ± 0.664 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000,000 loops each) In [6]: %timeit s.isascii() 11.5 ns ± 0.0159 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100,000,000 loops each) In [7]: %timeit s.isalpha() 79.2 ns ± 0.102 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000,000 loops each) ``` So `isascii` is about 20x faster than this regex. If we use `isascii`, we'll no longer identify control chars as width 0. But I think that might be the right call anyway, because many editors will display control-chars (e.g. here is VSCode on a file with all the ASCII-range characters): So I think we should stick with `isascii`.

> 3.3.4.1 Discussion—For the special condition of a horizon-
tal plane the hemispherical solar irradiance is properly termed
global solar irradiance, E G . Incorrectly, global tilted, or total
global irradiance is often used to indicate hemispherical
irradiance for a tilted plane. In case of a sun-tracking receiver,
this hemispherical irradiance is commonly called global nor-
mal irradiance. The adjective global should refer only to
hemispherical solar radiation on a horizontal, not a tilted,
surface.

Well, I ended up using weak references instead of phantom references. According to https://web.archive.org/web/20110303225354/http://weblogs.java.net/blog/2006/05/04/understanding-weak-references, I think there's no additional benefit in using phantom references. Besides, weak references are enqueued before finalization while phantom references are enqueued after. So we can remove local references of object refs earlier.

Trying to think of when an event might be unfetched:
- Calling `latest_materialization_records_by_key`, which will only fetch the latest event per asset. Future calls won't even pull up the skipped event because the cursor points to the latest evaluated event and any skipped event will be before that
- `materialization_records_for_key` is called sporadically and there are unfetched events in between each call. These events are presumably unpartitioned and since the user fetched updated events later, can't think of a reason why they would want old unfetched events
- User accidentally creates unpartitioned materialization for a partitioned asset. This event wouldn't show up in `latest_materialization_records_by_partition`/`latest_materialization_records_by_partition_and_asset`, but we could remedy this by also including `None` partitions in return value

I just found reason (b/135504433), it used to suppress the log message `Slave did not respond to frame`. When the RCP is during the restart stage, the `INT` pin may be set to low by default, it will continuously trigger the `spi-hdlc-adapter` to start SPI transaction. Because there is no response from the RCP during the RCP restart stage, the `spi-hdlc-adapter` will output lots of log message `Slave did not respond to frame`. Adding a delay could wait for the RCP starts, and then the `INT` pin will be in a definite state, and it won't trigger invalid SPI transactions.

So we still need to keep the delay. I will restore it back.

This is for actor reuse:

- We want to start 4 trials, but only 1 can run in parallel
- We request resources for 4 actors to potentially trigger autoscaling
- However, autoscaling either takes a long time or is disabled
- Trial 1 finishes and we cache the actor.
- We now want to start trial 2. We have a cached actor, so we use that
- The actor we originally requested for trial 2 is now not needed anymore (as trial 2 uses the cached actor), so we remove it

Generally actor reuse is faster than removing the old actor and starting a new one. This is especially important for short running trials, trials that are paused frequently, or when the Actor setup (e.g. imports) takes a long time.

> >Horable mention: Raylet kills processes that are in the Raylet process group but have ppid 1. It is not guaranteed that ppid==1 for orphaned processes.
> 
> Why isn't this a candidate solution? It seems to (1) be correct e2e, and (2) pretty straightforward to implement via polling, for example. Even if ppid != 1, we could do it for any unknown ppid.

I avoided that solution because you have to distinguish between leaked child processes and non-leaked child processes (since at depth>1 child processes will have unknown ppids). And climbing the ppid tree has race conditions which are especially risky if there are many child processes being created.

That said, we could do something like a mark-and-sweep approach to drastically reduce the chance of these race conditions; on the first poll, mark candidate processes for termination, on the second poll confirm the tree structure is the same and then kill the child process.

This still fails when the raylet crashes, since the entire old raylet process group will not be known.

I think this is potentially something we do before (2) above. But even this case will require the core-worker to kill child processes on exit to handle raylet crashes (which is the code in this PR).

There are 3 values in play here: `actual document id, None, []`
- When the actual document id is present, the idea is to call only that handle(for example `sync_solver_document`), so that would only get synced, and other sync functions are not called.
- When None values, the idea is to execute all the sync functions, so they can sync the all document present ceph.

- when [] is value, it just states, one of the handlers is having document_id. so executing it not all the functions should be executed. Only the handler with document_id.

```suggestion
    column_names = set(table.column_names)
    for field, value in partitions.items():
        if field in column_names:
            # Partition column exists in-data, validate that the in-data values are equal to the
            # partition value.
            import pyarrow.compute as pa
            
            # Cast partition value to column type.
            # TODO: Handle cast error.
            value = pa.scalar(value).cast(table.schema.field(field).type)
            # pac.equal() will return None if there are any null elements in the column,
            # so we explicitly check if the result is False instead of checking if its falsey.
            if pac.equal(value, table[field]) is False:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"Partition column {field} exists in table data, but partition value {value} "
                    f"is different from in-data values: {table[field].unique()}"
                )
        else:
            column = pa.array([value] * len(table))
            table = table.append_column(field, column)

let me provide a detailed example.

one of the worst things in unit testing that can happen, is a leakage of environment from one test to another
Let's take an example, based on proposed alternative implementation where one gets `.registry` attribute:
```python
import responses
from responses.registries import StrictOrderRegistry, PopFirstKeepLastRegistry

assert type(responses.mock.registry) == PopFirstKeepLastRegistry

with responses.RequestsMock() as rsps:
    rsps.registry = StrictOrderRegistry()
    assert type(rsps.registry) == StrictOrderRegistry


@responses.activate
def my_test_func():
    responses.mock.registry = StrictOrderRegistry()
    assert type(responses.mock.registry) == StrictOrderRegistry


my_test_func()
assert type(responses.mock.registry) == PopFirstKeepLastRegistry
# >>> AssertionError, since responses.mock.registry is equal to StrictOrderRegistry
```
what we see, that if anybody in some test will assign value of the registry, then it will propagate to all other tests.
we need to support identical behavior for both Context Manager and for decorator. Of course, we can revert to default registry on `__exit__`, but this will be:
1. implicit (I prefer to be explicit whenever it is possible) 
2. will cause issues when `responses.mock.registry` set outside of the function (globally), since this will be applied only to a first function executed

on top of this we have additional minor issue, user can make a typo in attribute name and still think that registry is set, while it is not. eg `responses.mock.regstry`

this leads that we need to set registry on each test, reasons for this:
1. explicit
2. allows user to precisely control behavior of tests

drawback that we need to do extra coding is eliminated with new proposal of **backwards compatible** decorator, that can take argument of registry `@responses.activate(registry=CustomRegistry)` or `registry` argument in context manager itself. Both ways should be easy to use.

Probably the biggest concern is this new default encoding. The reason keyring defaulted to UTF-16 was because that's the encoding that other tools (except Electron) appear to use UTF-16 by default. See #438 for more details. I'd like for the stored passwords to be interoperable where possible (i.e. a password stored by keyring should be readable by other programs). Obviously, they won't be interoperable if they are sharded, but this change alters the default encoding as well. And unfortunately, there's no standard here, just a popular convention. I don't think it will be advisable to change the default encoding to utf-8 until there's a substantial move to support that format in other tools. This change would also be backward incompatible in that it would make new passwords incompatible with older keyrings prior to support for UTF-8 decoding.
